content:
# Philippians Chapter 1 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Philippians 1 (Greetings, thanksgiving, imprisonment)

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "slaves" vs "servants" | "slaves of Christ Jesus" | "servants of Christ Jesus" | "servants of Christ Jesus" | **"servants"** — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree. While DOULOI literally means "slaves," "servants" avoids anachronistic English connotations of chattel slavery while preserving the sense of total allegiance. |
| 1 | "holy-ones" vs "saints" vs "holy people" | "all the holy-ones in Christ Jesus, the [ones] being in Philippoi" | "all the saints in Christ Jesus who are in Philippi" | "all God's holy people in Philippi" | **"all the saints in Christ Jesus who are in Philippi"** — LOGOS rendering is formally faithful without being stilted. PNEUMA's "God's" is an interpretive addition not in the Greek. EMET's hyphenated forms are too literal for consensus. |
| 1 | "overseers and servants" vs "overseers and deacons" | "overseers and servants" | "overseers and deacons" | "overseers and deacons" | **"overseers and deacons"** — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree. DIAKONOIS as "deacons" is functionally understood in English; EMET's "servants" is too generic and loses the technical sense. |
| 1 | Sentence structure / "From Paul" | "Paulos and Timotheos, slaves of Christ Jesus, to all..." | "Paul and Timothy, servants of Christ Jesus, to all..." | "From Paul and Timothy, servants of Christ Jesus — to all..." | **"Paul and Timothy, servants..."** — LOGOS form. PNEUMA's "From" is an addition; EMET's transliterated names (Paulos, Timotheos) are too literal for consensus. |
| 2 | "Grace to you and peace" vs "May grace and peace come to you" | "Grace to you and peace from God [the] Father of us..." | "Grace to you and peace from God our Father..." | "May grace and peace come to you from God our Father..." | **"Grace to you and peace from God our Father..."** — EMET and LOGOS share the verbless greeting structure faithful to the Greek. PNEUMA's "May...come to you" adds a verb not present in the text. |
| 3 | Restructured sentence | "I give-thanks to the God of me upon every remembrance of you" | "I give thanks to my God upon every remembrance of you" | "Every time you cross my mind, I thank God for you" | **"I give thanks to my God upon every remembrance of you"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA's paraphrase inverts the sentence structure. EMET's hyphenated compound and "God of me" is too literal. |
| 4 | "petition" vs "prayer" | "always in every petition of me on-behalf-of all of you, with joy the petition making" | "always in every prayer of mine for you all, making my prayer with joy" | "Whenever I pray for all of you, I pray with joy" | **"always in every prayer of mine for all of you, making my prayer with joy"** — LOGOS base with "all of you" (reflecting PANTWN UMWN). EMET's "petition" is overly narrow for DEESIS; PNEUMA restructures freely. |
| 5 | "partnership" vs "fellowship" vs "partners in spreading" | "the partnership of you unto the good-message" | "your fellowship in the gospel" | "you have been my partners in spreading the good news" | **"your partnership in the gospel"** — EMET's "partnership" (KOINWNIA) is more precise than LOGOS's "fellowship"; PNEUMA's expansion ("partners in spreading") adds meaning not in the text. |
| 5 | "good-message" vs "gospel" vs "good news" | "good-message" | "gospel" | "good news" | **"gospel"** — The traditional English rendering of EUAGGELION. Used consistently throughout. EMET's calque and PNEUMA's "good news" are both valid but "gospel" is the standard formal-equivalent term. |
| 6 | "having-been-persuaded" vs "being confident" vs "completely confident" | "having-been-persuaded [of] this very-thing, that the [one] having-begun-in you a good work will-bring-it-to-completion" | "being confident of this very thing, that he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion" | "I am completely confident of this: the God who started a good work in you will carry it through to completion on the day Christ Jesus returns" | **"being confident of this very thing, that he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion until the day of Christ Jesus"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "the God who" (interpretive expansion) and "returns" (not in the Greek). EMET is too hyphenated. |
| 7 | Structure and "heart" clause direction | "because-of the having me in the heart you" (preserves ambiguity) | "because I hold you in my heart" | "because you hold a special place in my heart" | **"because I hold you in my heart"** — LOGOS reading. The accusative UMAS most naturally functions as the object of Paul's holding. PNEUMA's "special place" adds interpretive weight. EMET preserves the ambiguity but at the cost of readability. |
| 7 | "bonds" vs "chains" | "bonds of me" | "my chains" | "in chains" | **"my chains"** — LOGOS. All three render DESMOIS similarly but with different possessive constructions. "My chains" is clear and faithful. |
| 7 | "fellow-sharers" vs "fellow partakers" vs "you share in this grace" | "fellow-sharers of me of the grace all of you being" | "you all being fellow partakers with me of grace" | "you share in this grace with me" | **"all of you being fellow partakers with me of grace"** — LOGOS base. Preserves the participial structure. PNEUMA simplifies; EMET's word order is too Greek. |
| 8 | "bowels" vs "affection" vs "tender affection" | "in [the] bowels of Christ Jesus" | "with the affection of Christ Jesus" | "with the tender affection of Christ Jesus" | **"with the deep affection of Christ Jesus"** — A mediating rendering. EMET's "bowels" is anatomically literal; LOGOS's "affection" loses the visceral force; PNEUMA's "tender affection" is close. "Deep affection" preserves emotional depth without anatomical distraction. |
| 8 | "For [my] witness [is] the God" vs "For God is my witness" vs "God himself can testify" | "For [my] witness [is] the God" | "For God is my witness" | "God himself can testify" | **"For God is my witness"** — LOGOS. Natural English word order. PNEUMA's "himself can testify" is paraphrastic; EMET follows Greek word order too rigidly. |
| 9 | "full-knowledge" vs "knowledge" vs "real knowledge" | "in full-knowledge and all perception" | "in knowledge and all discernment" | "in real knowledge and every kind of insight" | **"in knowledge and all discernment"** — LOGOS. EPIGNWSIS can mean "full knowledge" (EMET) but "knowledge" suffices. PNEUMA's "real knowledge" and "every kind of insight" are interpretive expansions. |
| 9 | "yet more and more" vs "still more and more" vs "keep growing richer and richer" | "yet more and more may-abound" | "may abound still more and more" | "will keep growing richer and richer" | **"may abound still more and more"** — LOGOS. Preserves the subjunctive mood. PNEUMA's "growing richer and richer" is a free paraphrase. |
| 10 | "to-test the things differing" vs "approve the things that are excellent" vs "discern what truly matters" | "unto the you to-test the [things] differing" | "so that you may approve the things that are excellent" | "so that you can discern what truly matters" | **"so that you may discern the things that are excellent"** — Blending PNEUMA's "discern" (DOKIMAZEIN can mean test/discern) with LOGOS's "the things that are excellent" (TA DIAPHERONTA). |
| 10 | "pure-ones and not-stumbling" vs "pure and blameless" vs "pure and blameless lives" | "you-may-be pure-ones and not-stumbling" | "you may be pure and blameless" | "live pure and blameless lives" | **"you may be pure and blameless"** — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on the adjective pair. EMET's "not-stumbling" is a more literal rendering of APROSKOPOI but "blameless" captures it well. |
| 11 | "having-been-filled" vs "having been filled" vs "overflowing" | "having-been-filled [with the] fruit of righteousness" | "having been filled with the fruit of righteousness" | "overflowing with the fruit of righteousness" | **"having been filled with the fruit of righteousness"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "overflowing" intensifies beyond PEPLERWMENOI; EMET agrees with LOGOS in sense. |
| 11 | "unto [the] glory" vs "to the glory" vs "bringing glory" | "unto [the] glory and praise of God" | "to the glory and praise of God" | "bringing glory and praise to God" | **"to the glory and praise of God"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA restructures the prepositional phrase into an active participle ("bringing") not in the Greek. |
| 12 | "To-know but I-want you, brothers" vs "Now I want you to know, brothers" vs "brothers and sisters" | "To-know but I-want you, brothers, that the [things] concerning me rather unto advancement..." | "Now I want you to know, brothers, that the things concerning me have turned out rather for the advancement..." | "Now I want you to know, brothers and sisters, that what has happened to me has actually helped advance the good news" | **"Now I want you to know, brothers, that the things concerning me have turned out rather for the advancement of the gospel"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "brothers and sisters" adds a gender-inclusive expansion; "brothers" (ADELPHOI) is the manuscript reading. PNEUMA's "actually helped advance" is paraphrastic. |
| 13 | "Praitorion" vs "praetorium" vs "imperial guard" | "manifest in Christ became in the whole Praitorion" | "have become manifest in Christ in the whole praetorium" | "clear to the entire imperial guard...because of Christ" | **"have become manifest in Christ throughout the whole praetorium"** — LOGOS base. EMET's Greek transliteration is inconsistent with English convention; PNEUMA interprets PRAITORIW as "imperial guard" (one possible meaning but not certain). Retaining "praetorium" preserves the ambiguity. |
| 14 | "the greater-number" vs "most" vs "most" | "the greater-number of the brothers" | "most of the brothers" | "most of the brothers and sisters" | **"most of the brothers in the Lord"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA again adds "and sisters." EMET is overly literal with "the greater-number." |
| 14 | "word of God" vs "the word" vs "God's word" | "the word of God to-speak" | "to speak the word without fear" | "to speak God's word fearlessly" | **"to speak the word of God without fear"** — EMET and PNEUMA include "of God" (TON LOGON TOU THEOU per Sinaiticus). LOGOS omits "of God." The Sinaiticus reading is followed. |
| 15 | "envy and strife" vs "envy and strife" vs "jealousy and rivalry" | "through envy and strife" | "from envy and strife" | "out of jealousy and rivalry" | **"from envy and strife"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "jealousy and rivalry" are near-synonyms but "envy and strife" (PHTHONON KAI ERIN) is more traditional and precise. |
| 15 | "are-proclaiming" vs "preach" vs "are preaching" | "the Christ are-proclaiming" | "preach Christ" | "are preaching Christ" | **"preach Christ"** — LOGOS. Simple present captures the ongoing action. |
| 16 | Sentence structure | "the-ones indeed out-of love, knowing that unto [the] defense of the good-message I-am-set" | "The latter do so out of love, knowing that I am appointed for the defense of the gospel" | "Those who act out of love know that I have been placed here to defend the good news" | **"The latter do so out of love, knowing that I am appointed for the defense of the gospel"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "placed here" adds locative specificity not in the Greek. EMET follows Greek word order. |
| 17 | "supposing affliction to-raise-up" vs "supposing to raise up affliction" vs "thinking they can stir up trouble" | "supposing affliction to-raise-up [against] the bonds of me" | "supposing to raise up affliction for me in my chains" | "thinking they can stir up trouble for me while I am in chains" | **"supposing to raise up affliction for me in my chains"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "stir up trouble" is too colloquial; EMET's word order is too Greek. |
| 18 | "What for?" vs "What then?" vs "But so what?" | "What for? Except that in-every way..." | "What then? Only that in every way..." | "But so what? Either way..." | **"What then? Only that in every way..."** — LOGOS. TI GAR is a standard rhetorical transition. PNEUMA's "But so what?" is too colloquial for a formal translation; EMET's "What for?" is awkward in English. |
| 18 | "But also I-will-rejoice" vs "Indeed, I will continue to rejoice" vs "Yes, I will keep on rejoicing" | "But also I-will-rejoice" | "Indeed, I will continue to rejoice" | "Yes, I will keep on rejoicing" | **"Indeed, I will continue to rejoice"** — LOGOS. Captures the emphatic ALLA KAI with appropriate register. |
| 19 | "salvation" vs "deliverance" vs "deliverance" | "this to-me will-turn-out unto salvation" | "this will turn out for my deliverance" | "this will lead to my deliverance" | **"this will turn out for my deliverance"** — LOGOS. SWTHRIAN can mean "salvation" or "deliverance"; in context (Paul's imprisonment), "deliverance" is apt. PNEUMA agrees in meaning. |
| 19 | "petition" vs "prayer" vs "prayers" | "the petition of you" | "your prayer" | "your prayers" | **"your prayer"** — LOGOS. Singular DEHSEWS. PNEUMA pluralizes. |
| 20 | "earnest-expectation" vs "eager expectation" vs "eagerly expect" | "the earnest-expectation and hope of me" | "my eager expectation and hope" | "I eagerly expect and hope" | **"my eager expectation and hope"** — LOGOS. Preserves the nominal construction of APOKARADOKIAN. PNEUMA converts to a verbal form. |
| 20 | "will-be-magnified Christ" vs "Christ will be magnified" vs "I will have the courage to exalt Christ" | "will-be-magnified Christ in the body of me" | "Christ will be magnified in my body" | "I will have the courage to exalt Christ in my body" | **"Christ will be magnified in my body"** — LOGOS. The passive MEGALUNTHESETAI ("will be magnified") is preserved. PNEUMA converts to active voice ("I will...exalt"), changing the theological nuance. |
| 21 | "the to-live [is] Christ" vs "to live is Christ" vs "living means Christ...pure gain" | "the to-live [is] Christ, and the to-die [is] gain" | "to live is Christ and to die is gain" | "living means Christ, and dying is pure gain" | **"to live is Christ, and to die is gain"** — LOGOS (with comma added). PNEUMA's "pure gain" adds an adjective not in the Greek. EMET's articular infinitive markers are too literal. |
| 22 | Structure and "fruit of work" vs "fruitful labor" | "If but the to-live in [the] flesh, this to-me [is the] fruit of work; and what I-will-choose, I-make-not-known" | "But if to live in the flesh — if this means fruitful labor for me, then what I shall choose I do not make known" | "But if I go on living in the body, that means fruitful work for me. So which would I choose? I honestly do not know" | **"But if I am to live in the flesh — this means fruitful labor for me; and what I shall choose, I do not make known"** — Mediating. LOGOS's "fruitful labor" captures KARPOS ERGOU well. PNEUMA adds "honestly" and restructures into questions. EMET's "fruit of work" is too literal. |
| 23 | "being-pressed-together" vs "hard pressed" vs "pulled in two directions" | "I-am-being-pressed-together but out-of the two" | "I am hard pressed between the two" | "I am pulled in two directions" | **"I am hard pressed between the two"** — LOGOS. SUNECHOMAI means "constrained/pressed." PNEUMA's "pulled in two directions" reverses the image from compression to tension. |
| 23 | "much more better" vs "far better" vs "far, far better" | "much more better" | "far better" | "far, far better" | **"far, far better"** — PNEUMA. The triple comparative POLLW MALLON KREISSON demands intensification. LOGOS's "far better" under-represents the stacking; EMET's "much more better" is ungrammatical in English though literally accurate. PNEUMA's doubling mirrors the Greek intensification. |
| 24 | "more-necessary" vs "more needful" vs "more necessary" | "the but to-remain in the flesh [is] more-necessary on-account-of you" | "yet to remain in the flesh is more needful on account of you" | "But it is more necessary for your sake that I remain in the body" | **"yet to remain in the flesh is more needful on account of you"** — LOGOS. "Needful" renders ANAGKAIOTERON with appropriate register. PNEUMA restructures and uses "body" for SARKI ("flesh"). |
| 25 | "having-been-persuaded, I-have-known" vs "being confident...I know" vs "Since I am convinced" | "this having-been-persuaded, I-have-known that I-will-remain and I-will-continue-with all of you unto the of-you advancement and joy of the faith" | "being confident of this, I know that I shall remain and continue with you all, for your progress and joy in the faith" | "Since I am convinced of this, I know I will stay and continue with all of you, so that you may grow and find joy in your faith" | **"And being confident of this, I know that I shall remain and continue with all of you, for your progress and joy in the faith"** — LOGOS base. PNEUMA's "grow" replaces "progress" (PROKOPHN); "stay" is too casual. |
| 26 | "boast" vs "boasting" vs "pride" | "the boast of you may-abound in Christ Jesus in me" | "your boasting may abound in Christ Jesus on account of me" | "your pride in Christ Jesus will overflow on my account" | **"your boasting may abound in Christ Jesus on account of me"** — LOGOS. KAUCHMA is "boast/boasting." PNEUMA's "pride" and "overflow" are free renderings. |
| 27 | "conduct-yourselves-as-citizens" vs "conduct yourselves in a manner worthy" vs "live your lives in a way that is worthy" | "Only worthily of the good-message of the Christ conduct-yourselves-as-citizens" | "Only conduct yourselves in a manner worthy of the gospel of Christ" | "Only this: live your lives in a way that is worthy of the good news of Christ" | **"Only conduct yourselves as citizens worthy of the gospel of Christ"** — Mediating. POLITEUESTHE specifically means "live as citizens." EMET captures this; LOGOS notes it but renders generically. The civic overtone is important for the Roman colony of Philippi. |
| 27 | "with-one-soul contending-together" vs "with one mind striving together" vs "fighting side by side with one purpose" | "with-one-soul contending-together for the faith of the good-message" | "with one mind striving together for the faith of the gospel" | "fighting side by side with one purpose for the faith of the good news" | **"striving together with one mind for the faith of the gospel"** — LOGOS base. MIAI PSUCHEI is "with one soul/mind." PNEUMA's "fighting side by side" is a free rendering of SUNATHOUNTES. |
| 28 | "proof" vs "sign" vs "sign" | "which is to-them [a] proof of destruction, but of-you of salvation" | "which is to them a sign of destruction, but to you of salvation" | "This is a sign to them of their destruction — but of your salvation" | **"which is to them a sign of destruction, but to you of salvation"** — LOGOS. ENDEIXIS can mean "proof" or "sign"; "sign" is well attested. PNEUMA adds possessives ("their," "your") not strictly in the Greek. |
| 28 | "not being-frightened in nothing" vs "not being frightened in anything" vs "not intimidated in any way" | "not being-frightened in nothing" | "not being frightened in anything" | "not intimidated in any way" | **"not being frightened in anything"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "intimidated" is interpretive; EMET's double negative is ungrammatical in English. |
| 29 | "it-was-granted" vs "it has been granted" vs "given the privilege" | "to you it-was-granted, the [thing] on-behalf-of Christ, not only the into him to-believe, but also the on-behalf-of him to-suffer" | "to you it has been granted on behalf of Christ, not only to believe in him, but also to suffer on his behalf" | "For you have been given the privilege, on behalf of Christ, not only of believing in him but also of suffering for him" | **"because to you it has been granted on behalf of Christ, not only to believe in him, but also to suffer on his behalf"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "given the privilege" is an interpretive expansion (though theologically apt). EMET's articular infinitive structure is too literal. |
| 30 | "such-as you-saw" vs "which you saw" vs "you once saw me face" | "the same struggle having, such-as you-saw in me and now you-hear [is] in me" | "having the same struggle which you saw in me and now hear to be in me" | "since you are going through the same struggle you once saw me face — and as you hear, I am facing still" | **"having the same struggle which you saw in me and now hear to be in me"** — LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "once," "face," and "still" — interpretive expansions. EMET's "such-as" is awkward. |

— SOPHIA
